{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding a driver earnings transparency feature across three jurisdictions (California, Germany, Indonesia) with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal requirements, and approval processes. This is not one feature—it is three jurisdiction-specific implementations sharing a common UI concept.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation / Earnings Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The fixed compensation a driver receives per shift or per hour, independent of delivery volume. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage. For contractors (ID), this is the base rate per engagement." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to each completed delivery. Distinct from base pay and must be displayed as a separate line item." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "Voluntary gratuity from a customer to a driver. In California, tips are the sole property of the driver and MUST NOT be counted toward minimum wage or base pay calculations." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from gross earnings. The nature of deductions differs by driver classification: for employees, these include taxes, benefits, and legally authorized withholdings; for independent contractors, these are platform fees, commissions, or equipment charges." },
      { "term": "Gross Earnings", "definition": "Sum of Base Pay + Per-Delivery Bonuses + Tips, before any deductions are applied." },
      { "term": "Net Earnings", "definition": "Gross Earnings minus all Deductions. The amount the driver actually receives." },
      { "term": "Itemized Wage Statement", "definition": "California Labor Code §226 requirement: a detailed breakdown including gross wages, total hours, piece rates, deductions, net wages, pay period, and employer information. Legally mandated for employees." },
      { "term": "Betriebsrat (Works Council)", "definition": "German employee representative body with codetermination rights. Under BetrVG §87(1)(6), the works council must approve any technical system that monitors employee performance or compensation display before deployment." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal employment status of the driver—either Employee or Independent Contractor—which determines applicable labor laws, deduction types, and transparency obligations." },
      { "term": "Earnings Period", "definition": "The time window over which earnings are aggregated for display. Must be clearly labeled (e.g., current shift, current day, current pay period)." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Displayed earnings components (Base Pay + Per-Delivery Bonuses + Tips - Deductions) must sum exactly to the displayed Net Earnings. No rounding discrepancies, no unexplained remainders.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Arithmetic integrity of financial data. If the breakdown does not sum to the total, the system is displaying corrupt or misleading financial information. This is a data consistency constraint that no policy change can override.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any driver at any point in time: BasePay + PerDeliveryBonus + Tips - SumOfDeductions == NetEarnings, verified to the cent."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "In California, every deduction must be individually itemized with a label and amount. Aggregate or unlabeled deductions are prohibited. This satisfies Labor Code §226 itemized wage statement requirements.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Legal mandate under California Labor Code §226. Failure to provide itemized deductions exposes the platform to statutory penalties of $50-$100 per pay period per employee, plus potential class action liability. This is not a policy choice—it is a legal constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For CA drivers, the deductions section contains zero entries with a generic label like 'Other' or 'Miscellaneous'; every deduction has a specific, human-readable category and a non-zero amount."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, tips must never be included in base pay calculations or used to offset minimum wage obligations. Tips must be displayed as a visually and logically separate line item from Base Pay.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California Labor Code §351 prohibits employers from crediting tips against minimum wage. Conflating tips with base pay—even in display—could constitute evidence of tip credit violations. This is a hard legal constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The base pay field value is identical whether or not the driver received any tips in the same period. Tips never appear within or adjacent to the base pay line item without clear visual separation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The earnings transparency feature MUST NOT be deployed in Germany until the applicable Betriebsrat has provided documented approval. The system must enforce a jurisdiction-level feature gate for Germany.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "BetrVG §87(1)(6) grants the works council codetermination rights over technical monitoring systems. Deploying without approval is a violation of German labor law and can result in injunctions, fines, and invalidation of the system. This is a hard legal gate, not a process preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "The feature flag for Germany returns disabled/blocked until an explicit works council approval record exists in the system. No German driver can access the earnings breakdown screen without this gate being cleared."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Each deduction displayed must reference the legal or contractual basis for the deduction (e.g., 'Income Tax Withholding - Federal', 'Platform Service Fee per Contractor Agreement §4').",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Best practice for transparency and trust, and practically required in California. In Indonesia, the contractual basis may suffice. A VP could adjust the level of detail required per jurisdiction, but the principle of explainability should hold.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every deduction line item includes a reference field that is non-empty and maps to either a legal statute or a contract clause identifier."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The earnings breakdown must clearly indicate the driver's classification (Employee or Independent Contractor) and the jurisdiction, as these determine which earnings rules, tax treatments, and deduction types apply.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Important for driver comprehension and audit trail, but a VP could decide to display this contextually or on a separate screen. The underlying data must always be correct, but the display location is a UX policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "The earnings view for a California driver shows classification as 'Employee'; for an Indonesian driver, 'Independent Contractor'. The jurisdiction is either displayed or inferrable from locale context."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Earnings data must reflect completed and confirmed deliveries only. Pending, disputed, or cancelled deliveries must not inflate displayed earnings.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying unconfirmed earnings as real earnings is financially misleading. If a driver sees $50 earned but only $30 is confirmed, they are making financial decisions on false data. This is a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The earnings total only includes deliveries with status = 'completed' or 'confirmed'. No delivery with status 'pending', 'disputed', or 'cancelled' contributes to the displayed total."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "In California, the system must display total hours worked alongside earnings, as required by the itemized wage statement mandate for employees.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Labor Code §226(a) requires total hours worked on itemized statements for non-exempt employees. Omitting hours from a system billing itself as an earnings transparency tool would be a compliance gap.",
      "testable_assertion": "For California employee drivers, the earnings view includes a 'Total Hours Worked' field that is non-null and greater than zero for any period with non-zero earnings."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A California driver has completed 5 deliveries in the current shift with base pay, bonuses, tips, and tax withholdings",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see separate line items for Base Pay, Per-Delivery Bonus (×5), Tips, each individual deduction with label, total hours worked, and a Net Earnings total that sums correctly to the cent"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The Germany works council has NOT yet approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "The feature is not visible or accessible; the driver sees their existing compensation view unchanged, with no partial or preview access"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The Germany works council HAS approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see the full breakdown appropriate for their jurisdiction, and the system logs the approval reference that enabled the feature"
    },
    {

      "then": "They see Base Pay, Per-Delivery Bonus, Tips, and platform fees/deductions with contract references, and their classification is shown as Independent Contractor"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",

      "when": "The system calculates and displays earnings",
      "then": "The base pay amount is independent of tips—identical to what it would be with $0 in tips—and tips appear as a fully separate line item"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver has 3 completed deliveries and 1 pending delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their real-time earnings",
      "then": "Only the 3 completed deliveries contribute to the earnings total; the pending delivery is either excluded or shown in a visually distinct 'pending' section that is not included in the totals"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A California driver's earnings breakdown is displayed",
      "when": "An auditor sums all displayed components (base + bonuses + tips - deductions)",
      "then": "The result equals the displayed Net Earnings to the exact cent, with zero discrepancy"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver works a shift but receives zero tips and has deductions exceeding base pay + bonuses", "expected_behavior": "Net earnings may display as negative. The system must display this honestly with a clear explanation of why the net is negative, not hide or clamp to zero. In California, negative net pay on a wage statement likely indicates an error in deduction logic that must be flagged." },
    { "scenario": "Customer adjusts or removes a tip after it was already displayed to the driver", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown must update to reflect the adjustment. The system should log the tip modification event and, if jurisdiction requires, notify the driver of the change. The previous tip amount must not persist in the displayed total." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is disputed or reversed after being shown as completed", "expected_behavior": "The earnings for that delivery are removed or moved to a 'disputed' state, and the totals are recalculated. The driver must be able to see what changed and why (audit trail)." },
    { "scenario": "Currency conversion for a multinational driver or cross-border delivery", "expected_behavior": "All amounts displayed in the local currency of the driver's jurisdiction. If conversion occurred, the original amount and exchange rate should be available for transparency." },
    { "scenario": "Germany works council approval is revoked after feature rollout", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately disabled for all German drivers. The feature gate must support both enabling and disabling. Historical data already viewed is not retracted, but the live feature is turned off." },
    { "scenario": "Driver classified as employee in California switches to a jurisdiction where they would be a contractor", "expected_behavior": "The earnings display must reflect the new classification and applicable rules from the effective date of the change. Historical earnings remain displayed under the rules that applied at the time they were earned." },
    { "scenario": "Real-time display during a delivery in progress (driver is mid-route)", "expected_behavior": "Base pay and any time-based accrual may update in real time, but per-delivery bonus and tips must only appear upon delivery completion. Partial or speculative earnings must not be shown as confirmed." },
    { "scenario": "Platform fee percentage changes mid-pay-period for Indonesian contractors", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries completed before the change use the old fee rate; deliveries after use the new rate. Both rates must be visible in the deduction breakdown, not blended." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "High compliance cost but high risk mitigation. California non-compliance with §226 carries penalties of $50-$100 per violation per pay period, compounding to millions in class actions. Germany non-compliance risks injunctions. The feature itself has near-zero revenue impact but significant liability reduction. Implementation cost spans three jurisdiction-specific variants.",
    "operational": "Significant operational complexity: requires jurisdiction-aware feature gating, works council approval workflows, classification-aware deduction logic, and real-time earnings calculation pipelines.     "user": "High positive impact on driver trust and retention. Earnings transparency is consistently cited as a top driver concern in gig economy platforms.     "risk": "PRIMARY RISK: Displaying incorrect earnings data is worse than displaying no data. A wrong breakdown erodes trust faster than opacity.     "stakeholders_affected": [

      { "group": "German Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "impact": "Must be engaged as a first-class stakeholder. Has legal authority to block, modify, or impose conditions on the feature. Failure to consult early is both illegal and relationship-damaging." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must review and sign off on each jurisdiction's implementation. Ongoing burden to monitor legislative changes. California wage statement requirements are litigated frequently." },
      { "group": "Finance/Payroll", "impact": "The displayed data must reconcile with actual payroll disbursements. Any discrepancy between what drivers see and what they receive will generate disputes and potential legal exposure." },
